days.title: Your First Chord: E Major
days.focus: Building the E major chord and making every string ring
days.intro: Week 2 begins with the big one: your first chord. E major uses all six strings, sounds huge, and once you own it, thousands of songs move within reach. Take it slow — today is about clean, not fast.

steps.title: Tune and warm up
steps.instructions: Tune all 6 strings, then do one round of the 1-2-3 fret walk on the thin E string to wake up your fretting fingers.

steps.title: Build the E chord finger by finger
steps.instructions: Place your index finger on the G string, 1st fret. Add your middle finger on the A string, 2nd fret. Add your ring finger on the D string, 2nd fret, tucked just below your middle finger. Fingertips on their tips, thumb behind the neck, each finger close behind its fret. Take the whole shape off and rebuild it 5 times — building the shape from nothing is the real skill.

steps.title: The string-by-string check
steps.instructions: Hold the chord and pick each of the 6 strings one at a time, slowly. Every string should ring clearly — no buzzes, no dead thuds. If a string is muted, a neighboring finger is probably leaning on it; re-curl your fingers so they come down vertically. Do the full 6-string check 4 times.

steps.title: Strum it
steps.instructions: Give the E chord 10 slow, full downstrums, counting '1, 2, 3, 4.' Between every couple of strums, lift your hand off and rebuild the shape. That's your first chord — let it ring and enjoy it.

youtube.title: The 8 Essential Beginner Chord Grips | Beginner Guitar Lesson 8.1 | Justin Guitar

tips: Squeeze only as hard as needed for a clean note — a death grip tires your hand and slows changes later.
A slightly muffled chord today is fine. Clean comes from repetition, not force.

days.title: Chord Two: A Major
days.focus: Building the A major chord with three fingers in one fret
days.intro: Chord number two: A major. Three fingers squeeze into the second fret, which feels crowded at first — everyone's hand complains for a day or two, then it clicks. With E and A you'll already have a two-chord vocabulary.

steps.title: Tune and E chord warm-up
steps.instructions: Tune up, then build the E chord from nothing 5 times, strumming once each time. Run one string-by-string check to make sure it still rings.

steps.title: Build the A chord
steps.instructions: Line up index, middle, and ring fingers on the 2nd fret of the D, G, and B strings — in a row, one string each. Angle your fingers slightly so all three fit behind the fret; it's a tight squeeze and that's normal. The thick E string stays out of this chord, so don't strum it. Build the shape from nothing 5 times.

steps.title: String-by-string check
steps.instructions: Pick each string from the A string (5th) down to the thin E, one at a time. The most common problem is the thin E getting muted by the underside of your ring finger — re-curl so your fingertips come straight down. Do the check 4 times, fixing one problem string at a time.

steps.title: Strum A, visit E
steps.instructions: Strum the A chord 4 times (skipping the thick E string), counting steadily. Then switch to the E chord and strum it 4 times. Alternate like this for the rest of the step — no rush on the switch, just get there cleanly. That switch is tomorrow's whole lesson.

youtube.title: How to Play the A Chord | Guitar for Beginners

tips: If three fingers won't fit side by side, try stacking them in a slight diagonal line.
Hitting the muted low E occasionally is fine for now — your strumming aim improves with time.

days.title: E to A: Your First Chord Change
days.focus: Switching between E and A, slow motion then one-minute changes
days.intro: Knowing chords is one thing; switching between them in time is what makes music. Today you start the single most valuable practice habit in this whole course: one-minute changes. Count your score — you'll beat it all week.

steps.title: Tune and chord check
steps.instructions: Tune up, then build E and A each 3 times from nothing with a string-by-string check on one of each. Clean shapes first, speed later.

steps.title: Slow-motion changes
steps.instructions: Hold E, strum once, then move to A in deliberate slow motion — watch each finger travel to its new string. Notice the shortcut: your index and middle fingers barely move between these shapes. Strum A once, then crawl back to E. Do 10 round trips at whatever speed keeps the movement smooth and tension-free.

steps.title: One-minute changes
steps.instructions: Set a 60-second timer. Switch E-A-E-A as many times as you can, strumming once per chord — every arrival counts as one change, even if it's scrappy. Write down your score. Rest 30 seconds, then do two more one-minute rounds and try to beat it. Anything above 10 is a solid first day; the number only goes up from here.

steps.title: Cool-down strums
steps.instructions: Finish with 4 slow strums on E and 4 on A, making them as clean and ringing as you can. End on a good sound.

youtube.title: One Minute Changes (Guitar Lesson BC-115) Guitar for beginners Stage 1

tips: During one-minute changes, scrappy is allowed — the drill trains speed, your slow practice trains clean.
Move all fingers toward the new chord at once, not one at a time.
Log today's score. Beating your own number is addictive in the best way.

days.title: Chord Three: D Major
days.focus: Building the D major chord on the top four strings
days.intro: Meet D major — the triangle-shaped chord that completes the legendary E-A-D trio. Master these three and you can play thousands of real songs, including the ones coming next week. D only uses four strings, so your strumming aim gets a workout too.

steps.title: Tune and warm up
steps.instructions: Tune up, then do one 60-second round of E-A one-minute changes. Check today's count against yesterday's score.

steps.title: Build the D chord
steps.instructions: Index finger on the G string, 2nd fret. Middle finger on the thin E string, 2nd fret. Ring finger on the B string, 3rd fret — it sits below the other two, forming a little triangle. Only the thinnest four strings get played; the low E and A stay silent. Build it from nothing 6 times, saying the finger positions out loud as you place them.

steps.title: String-by-string check
steps.instructions: Pick the four strings one at a time: open D, then G, B, and thin E. The usual culprit is the thin E muted by your ring finger's underside — arch that finger higher. Run the check 4 times until all four ring.

steps.title: Strum practice with aim
steps.instructions: Strum just the top four strings, 10 slow downstrums. Start your strum from the open D string — aim is approximate at first, and grazing the A string won't ruin anything. Rebuild the shape from nothing between every 2 strums.

youtube.title: Super Easy First Guitar Lesson - Guitar Lessons For Beginners - Stage 1 - The D Chord

tips: Think 'triangle' — if the shape looks like a triangle pointing at you, you've got it.
D sounds thin compared to E. That's the chord's character, not your mistake.

days.title: A to D: Changes with an Anchor
days.focus: Switching between A and D using minimal finger movement
days.intro: Today you drill the A-to-D change and learn the pro secret: fingers that travel less arrive faster. This pair shares neighborhood real estate on the 2nd fret, so there's a shortcut waiting for you.

steps.title: Tune and chord recap
steps.instructions: Tune up, then build A and D each 3 times from nothing, with one string-by-string check apiece. Both should feel familiar by now.

steps.title: Find the shortcut
steps.instructions: Make the A chord, then change to D in slow motion and watch your fingers: your index finger stays on the same fret, just hopping one string. Do 5 ultra-slow changes focusing only on moving your fingers the shortest possible distance. Less travel means faster changes — this idea applies to every chord pair you'll ever learn.

steps.title: Slow changes with strums
steps.instructions: Strum A once, change to D, strum once, change back. Do 10 round trips at a relaxed pace, keeping each chord clean. Remember your strum aim: 5 strings for A, 4 for D.

steps.title: One-minute changes: A-D
steps.instructions: Set a 60-second timer and count your A-D changes, one strum per chord. Do 2 rounds with a 30-second rest between, and log your best score. Then do one final round of E-A changes to keep yesterday's pair warm.

youtube.title: A, D and E Chords - Easy Chord Changes Using Anchor Fingers - Beginner Guitar Lessons [BC-114]

tips: Prepare the change in your head one strum early — think 'D' while still strumming A.
Keep fingers hovering low over the strings between chords; high fingers are slow fingers.

days.title: Three Chords in Time
days.focus: Cycling E, A, and D with steady quarter-note strumming
days.intro: Today everything merges: all three chords, played in a loop, with strums that land on a steady count. This is the exact skeleton of real rhythm guitar — from here on out, you're not doing exercises, you're playing music.

steps.title: Tune and quick changes
steps.instructions: Tune up, then do one 60-second round of one-minute changes on your weakest pair from this week. Log the score.

steps.title: Quarter-note strums on one chord
steps.instructions: Hold E and strum on every count: '1, 2, 3, 4' — four even downstrums, then repeat without pausing. Keep your arm swinging like a pendulum, same speed every strum. Do 8 bars on E, then 8 on A, then 8 on D. The count never stops, even when your brain wants to.

steps.title: The three-chord cycle
steps.instructions: Play 4 strums on E, 4 on A, 4 on D, then back to E — around and around. Change chords during the small gap after count 4; it's fine to be late at first, but don't stop counting. Aim for 6 full cycles. If a change keeps derailing you, drop to 2 slow round trips of just that pair, then rejoin the cycle.

steps.title: Smooth it out
steps.instructions: Run the cycle 4 more times, slightly slower than feels necessary, and try to keep the strumming completely even through every change. A steady wobble-free loop at slow speed is the win today. Finish with one big final E strum and let it ring.

youtube.title: Your FIRST Strumming Pattern EVER! | Guitar for Beginners

tips: The count is king. A late chord with a steady count sounds better than a perfect chord that stops the music.
Slow enough that you never stop — that's the right tempo.

days.title: Week 2 Review: Three Chords Down
days.focus: Consolidating E, A, D and chord changes — plus your weekly recording
days.intro: Two weeks ago you'd never held a pick; today you can play the three most useful chords in guitar. This review day is lighter on the hands and big on payoff — including recording number two, which is going to sound noticeably better than last week's.

steps.title: Tune and chord inspection
steps.instructions: Tune up, then build E, A, and D once each with a full string-by-string check. Fix any dead strings before moving on — clean shapes make everything else easier.

steps.title: One-minute change medley
steps.instructions: One 60-second round each of E-A and A-D changes, with a short rest between. Compare against your logged scores from days 10 and 12 — most people jump 30-50% in the same week. Log today's numbers.

steps.title: The three-chord cycle, for fun
steps.instructions: Run yesterday's loop — 4 strums each on E, A, D — for 6 relaxed cycles. Pick your favorite chord to end on and give it 4 extra strums. This groove is your week's trophy.

steps.title: Record your 60 seconds
steps.instructions: Hit the Record button in the app and play about 60 seconds: the three-chord cycle, plus a bonus riff from week 1 if there's time. One take, mistakes and all. Then listen to your day-7 recording back to back with today's — hear the difference two weeks makes.

youtube.title: JustinGuitar Beginners Course - Practice Routine Grade 1

tips: Fourteen straight days of guitar — that's a real habit now. Protect it.
Sore fingertips should be easing this week as calluses arrive on schedule.
